title: A Class of Multipartite Entangled States Based on State Transitions
abstract: We introduce Transition states (T states), denoted by $\ket{T_k^n}$, as a class of multipartite entangled states characterized by a fixed number of state transitions between adjacent qubits. These states form equal-amplitude superpositions over all states with a specified transition count. Unlike Bell states based on two-qubit correlations, GHZ states characterized by global correlations among all qubits, and W and Dicke states based on fixed numbers of qubit excitations, T states are defined by transition counts along an ordered sequence of qubits. We prove that T states are unitarily equivalent to Dicke states through a chain of CX (controlled-X) operations, thereby establishing a direct correspondence between transition-based and excitation-based representations of multipartite entanglement.
summary: This paper introduces Transition states (T states), a novel class of multipartite entangled quantum states defined by the count of state transitions between adjacent qubits. The authors prove that these T states are mathematically equivalent to Dicke states, which are characterized by a fixed number of excitations. This establishes a powerful new structural link between two distinct ways of quantifying entanglement in multi-qubit systems.
main_problem: The paper introduces a new class of multipartite entangled states, Transition states (T states), defined by the number of state transitions between adjacent qubits.
main_result: It proves that T states are unitarily equivalent to Dicke states, establishing a direct structural correspondence between transition-based and excitation-based entanglement representations.
method: The characterization involves defining T states as equal-amplitude superpositions over basis states with a fixed number of adjacent transitions, followed by proving the unitary transformation linking them to Dicke states via CX operations.
model_or_system: The system is an $n$-qubit quantum system. The analysis compares T states (defined by transition counts) against established classes like Bell, GHZ, W, and Dicke states (defined by fixed Hamming weight/excitations).
key_observables: Number of state transitions between adjacent qubits; total number of excitations ($k$); entanglement structure.
important_parameters: The number of qubits ($n$) and the specified count for either transitions or excitations ($k$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes an ordered sequence of qubits to define adjacency for transition counting, which distinguishes T states from permutation-invariant Dicke states.
paper_structure: The paper first defines the novel T states based on sequential transitions. It then compares these states mathematically and physically against existing entanglement measures (Bell, GHZ, W, Dicke). The core of the work is proving the unitary equivalence between the two classes.
why_interesting: While focused on abstract quantum information theory, establishing structural equivalences between different physical constraints (like local transitions vs. global excitation numbers) provides powerful mathematical tools applicable to modeling complex many-body correlations in AMO systems or analyzing error correction codes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
